message:
fix(checkpoint): add milliseconds to IDs to prevent collisions

The checkpoint ID format now includes milliseconds (YYYYMMDD-HHMMSS.mmm)
instead of just seconds. This prevents ID collisions when multiple
checkpoints are created in rapid succession, which commonly occurs in
automated scenarios such as:

- Auto-checkpoint before batch operations
- Scripted session management
- CI/CD pipelines with rapid agent cycling

The ID length increases from 15 to 19 characters (without name) or
22 to 26 characters (with name), but this is acceptable given the
robustness improvement for automated workflows.

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.5 <noreply@anthropic.com>

 {"id":"ntm-pp5.3","title":"Prompt Templates with Variables","description":"Named templates with variable substitution for reusable prompts.

## Overview
Templates are named, parameterized prompts stored in files.
Users can invoke them via `ntm send --template=name`.

## Storage Locations
1. Global: ~/.config/ntm/templates/*.md
2. Project: .ntm/templates/*.md (if Per-Project Config enabled)

## Template Format
```markdown
---
name: code_review
description: Review code for quality issues
variables:
  file: File path to review
  focus: What to focus on (optional)
---

Review the file {{file}} for:
- Code quality issues
- Potential bugs
- Performance concerns
{{#focus}}
Focus especially on: {{focus}}
{{/focus}}

Be thorough but concise.
```

## Variable Types

### Required Variables
`{{variable}}` - Must be provided via --var

### Optional Variables (with conditionals)
`{{#variable}}...{{/variable}}` - Section only included if variable provided

### Built-in Variables
| Variable | Value |
|----------|-------|
| `{{file}}` | Content of --file argument |
| `{{files}}` | Content of all --file arguments |
| `{{selection}}` | Current editor selection (if available) |
| `{{clipboard}}` | Clipboard content |
| `{{cwd}}` | Current working directory |
| `{{session}}` | Session name |
| `{{date}}` | Current date |
| `{{time}}` | Current time |

## Command Interface
```bash
# Use template
ntm send myproject --template=code_review --var file=src/auth.py

# Use with file injection
ntm send myproject -t code_review --file=src/auth.py
# {{file}} auto-populated with file contents

# List available templates
ntm template list

# Show template content
ntm template show code_review

# Create new template
ntm template create my_template
# Opens editor with scaffold
```

## Template Resolution
1. Look in project templates (.ntm/templates/)
2. Look in global templates (~/.config/ntm/templates/)
3. Look in built-in templates (embedded in binary)

## Built-in Templates
Ship with sensible defaults:
- code_review: Review code for issues
- explain: Explain how code works
- refactor: Refactor code
- test: Write tests
- document: Add documentation

## Parsing Implementation
```go
func ParseTemplate(content string, vars map[string]string) (string, error) {
    // Parse frontmatter
    meta, body := splitFrontmatter(content)
    
    // Check required variables
    for _, v := range meta.Variables {
        if _, ok := vars[v.Name]; \!ok && v.Required {
            return "", fmt.Errorf("missing required variable: %s", v.Name)
        }
    }
    
    // Expand conditionals
    body = expandConditionals(body, vars)
    
    // Substitute variables
    body = substituteVariables(body, vars)
    
    return body, nil
}
```

## Files to Create
- internal/templates/loader.go - Find and load templates
- internal/templates/parser.go - Parse template syntax
- internal/templates/builtin.go - Embedded default templates
- internal/cli/template.go - Template management commands","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"feature","assignee":"RedCreek","created_at":"2025-12-10T02:41:04.28723-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-10T10:08:56.501011-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-10T10:08:56.501011-05:00","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"ntm-pp5.3","depends_on_id":"ntm-pp5","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2025-12-10T02:41:04.287592-05:00","created_by":"daemon"}]}
 {"id":"ntm-pp5.4","title":"History Command (ntm history)","description":"CLI command to view and search prompt history.

## Command Interface
```bash
ntm history                          # Show recent prompts
ntm history --session=myproject      # Filter by session
ntm history --limit=50               # Show last 50
ntm history --since=1h               # Last hour
ntm history --search='auth'          # Search prompt text
ntm history --json                   # Output as JSON
ntm history clear                    # Clear all history
ntm history clear --before=7d        # Clear older than 7 days
```

## Default Output
```
 #  TIME        SESSION      TARGETS     PROMPT
 1  10:31:00    myproject    cc_1,cc_2   implement JWT authentication for the API...
 2  10:30:00    myproject    cod_1       run all tests and fix any failures that...
 3  10:25:00    backend      gmi_1       analyze this codebase for security vulne...
 4  10:20:00    myproject    all (5)     review the changes and suggest improveme...
 5  10:15:00    frontend     cc_1        create a React component for user login...

Showing 5 of 127 entries. Use --limit for more.
```

## Detailed View
```bash
ntm history show 1
```
```
ID:       01HXYZ...
Time:     2025-12-10 10:31:00
Session:  myproject
Targets:  cc_1, cc_2
Source:   cli
Template: (none)

Prompt:
  implement JWT authentication for the API. Include:
  - Token generation
  - Token validation
  - Refresh token flow
  - Middleware for protected routes

Status: Success
```

## Flags
| Flag | Description |
|------|-------------|
| --limit, -n | Number of entries (default: 20) |
| --session, -s | Filter by session name |
| --since | Time filter (1h, 1d, 1w) |
| --search | Search prompt text |
| --source | Filter by source (cli, palette, replay) |
| --json | JSON output |

## Subcommands
- `ntm history` - List (default)
- `ntm history show <id>` - Show details
- `ntm history clear` - Clear history
- `ntm history export` - Export to file

## Implementation
```go
func newHistoryCmd() *cobra.Command {
    cmd := &cobra.Command{
        Use:   "history",
        Short: "View prompt history",
        RunE:  runHistoryList,
    }
    
    cmd.AddCommand(
        &cobra.Command{Use: "show", RunE: runHistoryShow},
        &cobra.Command{Use: "clear", RunE: runHistoryClear},
        &cobra.Command{Use: "export", RunE: runHistoryExport},
    )
    
    return cmd
}
```

 {"id":"ntm-qxp","title":"Implement command plugins as executable scripts","description":"Allow users to add custom commands via executable scripts.

## What This Task Does

Enable users to create custom NTM commands by placing executable
scripts in ~/.config/ntm/commands/. Any executable becomes a subcommand.

## Plugin Location

~/.config/ntm/commands/
├── sync-all*         # ntm sync-all
├── morning-setup*    # ntm morning-setup  
└── deploy*           # ntm deploy

## Script Interface

Scripts receive NTM context via environment variables:
- NTM_SESSION: Current/specified session name
- NTM_JSON: Set to '1' if --json flag passed
- NTM_CONFIG_PATH: Path to NTM config file
- NTM_VERSION: NTM version string

Scripts can use ntm commands internally:
#\!/bin/bash
# ~/.config/ntm/commands/sync-all
for session in $(ntm list --json | jq -r '.[].name'); do
  ntm send "$session" --all "git pull && npm install"
done

## Command Discovery

On startup:
1. Scan ~/.config/ntm/commands/
2. Check each file is executable
3. Register as cobra subcommand
4. Extract description from script header comment

Script description convention:
#\!/bin/bash
# Description: Sync all agents with latest code
# Usage: ntm sync-all [--force]

## Integration with Help

$ ntm --help
...
Plugin Commands:
  sync-all      Sync all agents with latest code
  morning-setup Set up morning development session
  deploy        Deploy current branch to staging

$ ntm sync-all --help
# Runs: script --help

## Arguments and Flags

Pass through all arguments to script:
$ ntm deploy --env staging --force
# Runs: ~/.config/ntm/commands/deploy --env staging --force

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Executables in commands/ become subcommands
- [ ] Environment variables passed to scripts
- [ ] Arguments passed through
- [ ] Description parsed from header
- [ ] Help shows plugin commands
- [ ] Non-executable files ignored with warning

 {"id":"ntm-vr2","title":"SUB-EPIC: E2E Tests with Detailed Logging","description":"# SUB-EPIC: End-to-End (E2E) Tests with Detailed Logging

## Scope
Complete workflow tests that exercise NTM from CLI invocation to tmux state verification.

## E2E Test Scenarios

### Scenario 1: Full Agent Workflow
1. ntm spawn project --cc=2 --cod=1
2. Verify session created with 4 panes (3 agents + 1 user)
3. ntm send project --cc 'hello world'
4. Verify prompt delivered to Claude panes only
5. ntm interrupt project
6. Verify Ctrl+C sent
7. ntm kill -f project
8. Verify session destroyed

### Scenario 2: Command Palette Workflow
1. Create session with agents
2. Invoke palette (programmatic, not TUI)
3. Select command
4. Verify prompt sent to targets

### Scenario 3: Quick Setup Workflow
1. ntm quick testproject --template=go
2. Verify directory structure created
3. Verify git initialized
4. Verify .vscode and .claude dirs created

### Scenario 4: Session Persistence
1. Create session, add agents
2. Detach
3. Re-attach
4. Verify state preserved

### Scenario 5: Robot Mode Output
1. Create various session states
2. Run --robot-status, --robot-plan
3. Verify JSON output matches actual state
4. Parse and validate JSON schema

## Logging Requirements

### Per-Test Log File
```
test_logs/
├── TestFullAgentWorkflow_20251210_103045.log
├── TestCommandPaletteWorkflow_20251210_103047.log
└── ...
```

### Log Content Structure
```
[2025-12-10T10:30:45Z] TEST START: TestFullAgentWorkflow
[2025-12-10T10:30:45Z] SETUP: Creating test session 'ntm_test_abc123'
[2025-12-10T10:30:45Z] EXEC: ntm spawn ntm_test_abc123 --cc=2 --cod=1
[2025-12-10T10:30:45Z] STDOUT: Session created: ntm_test_abc123 (4 panes)
[2025-12-10T10:30:45Z] VERIFY: Checking pane count...
[2025-12-10T10:30:45Z] ASSERT PASS: pane_count == 4
[2025-12-10T10:30:46Z] EXEC: ntm send ntm_test_abc123 --cc 'hello'
...
[2025-12-10T10:30:48Z] TEARDOWN: Killing session ntm_test_abc123
[2025-12-10T10:30:48Z] TEST END: PASS (3.2s)
```

### System State Capture
Before and after each test:
- tmux list-sessions
- tmux list-panes (for test session)
- Environment variables
- Working directory

## Implementation
- tests/e2e/ directory for all E2E tests
- Shared test harness with logging
- CI-compatible (skip if tmux unavailable)
- Parallel execution with unique session names","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2025-12-10T03:18:52.553331-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-10T03:18:52.553331-05:00","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"ntm-vr2","depends_on_id":"ntm-l5h","type":"blocks","created_at":"2025-12-10T03:19:06.044553-05:00","created_by":"daemon"},{"issue_id":"ntm-vr2","depends_on_id":"ntm-7lk","type":"blocks","created_at":"2025-12-10T03:19:06.232148-05:00","created_by":"daemon"}]}

 {"id":"ntm-vum","title":"EPIC: MCP Agent Mail Integration","description":"# MCP Agent Mail Integration

## Background & Motivation

NTM orchestrates multiple AI coding agents (Claude, Codex, Gemini) in parallel tmux panes. Currently, these agents operate in isolation - they can see the same filesystem but cannot:
- Communicate with each other directly
- Know what others are working on
- Coordinate file access to avoid conflicts
- Share status or hand off work

MCP Agent Mail provides exactly this coordination layer.

## CRITICAL CLARIFICATION: NTM's Role

**NTM is a COORDINATOR and BRIDGE, not an agent replacement.**

The agents running in panes (Claude Code, Codex, etc.) may or may not have their own Agent Mail integration:
- If they DO have Agent Mail support: NTM provides unified visibility (aggregate inbox, lock status)
- If they DON'T: NTM acts as coordinator, sending messages on behalf of the user

**Key insight**: NTM doesn't pretend to BE the agents. It's the orchestration layer that:
1. Provides a unified view of all agent mail activity
2. Allows the USER to send messages to/between agents
3. Shows file reservation status across all agents
4. Detects conflicts before spawning new agents

## What NTM's Agent Mail Integration Provides

### 1. Visibility Layer
- Aggregate inbox view: See messages across all registered agents
- File lock status: Who has reserved what files
- Agent directory: Which agents are active in the project

### 2. User-to-Agent Communication
```bash
ntm mail send myproject --to cc_1 "Please focus on the API endpoint"
```
This sends FROM the NTM coordinator TO the specified agent. It's the user speaking through NTM.

### 3. Session Coordination
```bash
ntm lock myproject "src/api/**" --reason "User-directed refactor"
```
NTM can reserve files on behalf of the session, signaling to all agents that these files are being coordinated.

### 4. Conflict Prevention
```bash
ntm spawn myproject --cc=2
# Warning: src/api/** is reserved by GreenCastle (cc_1 in session 'other')
# Proceed? [y/N]
```

## Technical Approach

MCP Agent Mail runs as an HTTP server (default port 8765) with JSON-RPC 2.0.

NTM will:
1. **Create internal/agentmail package**: HTTP client for Agent Mail MCP tools
2. **Register as coordinator**: `ntm_<session>` identity for user-initiated actions
3. **Aggregate views**: Fetch inbox/locks from all known agents
4. **Expose commands**: ntm mail, ntm lock, ntm locks
5. **Dashboard integration**: Show mail and lock status
6. **Graceful degradation**: All features optional if Agent Mail unavailable

## Key Design Decisions

- **NTM is a coordinator**: Sends messages as `ntm_<session>`, not as individual agents
- **Agents own their mail**: If Claude Code has Agent Mail support, it handles its own inbox
- **NTM aggregates and visualizes**: Unified view across all project agents
- **Optional integration**: Works without Agent Mail; features degrade gracefully
- **No duplication**: Don't re-implement what agents already do

## What This Is NOT

- NOT a replacement for agent-side Agent Mail integration
- NOT pretending NTM "is" the Claude/Codex/Gemini agent
- NOT required for basic NTM functionality

## Success Criteria

- [ ] NTM can detect if Agent Mail server is running
- [ ] NTM registers coordinator identity per session
- [ ] Aggregate inbox shows messages across all project agents
- [ ] File reservation status visible in status/dashboard
- [ ] Conflicts detected before spawning in reserved areas
- [ ] Graceful degradation when Agent Mail unavailable","status":"open","priority":0,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2025-12-10T03:17:05.78207-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-10T03:33:21.628729-05:00"}
 {"id":"ntm-vz9","title":"Implement ntm copy command with filtering","description":"Copy pane content to system clipboard with filtering options.

## What This Task Does

Create 'ntm copy' command to copy pane output to system clipboard.

## Interface

# Copy entire pane output
$ ntm copy myproject:1

# Copy last N lines
$ ntm copy myproject:1 --last 50

# Copy lines matching pattern
$ ntm copy myproject:1 --pattern 'ERROR.*'

# Copy only code blocks (markdown fences)
$ ntm copy myproject:1 --code

# Copy to file instead of clipboard
$ ntm copy myproject:1 --output result.txt

# Quiet mode (no confirmation message)
$ ntm copy myproject:1 --quiet

## Content Filtering

--last N: Capture last N lines of pane output
--pattern: Filter to lines matching regex
--code: Extract markdown code blocks only

Code block extraction:
```python
def hello():
    print('world')
```

Becomes:
def hello():
    print('world')

Multiple code blocks concatenated with newlines.

## Implementation

1. Capture pane content (existing CapturePaneOutput)
2. Apply filters in order:
   a. --last (if specified)
   b. --pattern (if specified)
   c. --code (if specified)
3. Copy to clipboard or file
4. Show confirmation unless --quiet

## JSON Output

$ ntm copy myproject:1 --json
{
  "source": "myproject:1",
  "lines": 42,
  "bytes": 1234,
  "destination": "clipboard"
}

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Basic copy to clipboard works
- [ ] --last N limits lines
- [ ] --pattern filters by regex
- [ ] --code extracts code blocks
- [ ] --output writes to file
- [ ] --quiet suppresses output
- [ ] --json shows copy metadata

 {"id":"ntm-w6p","title":"Unit Tests: internal/config package (16.2% → 80%)","description":"# Unit Tests: internal/config Package

## Current State
- Coverage: 16.2%
- Tests: 5 existing (default, getProjectDir, tilde expansion, nonexistent, categories)

## Test Approach
Use real temp files and directories. No mocks.

## Test Cases

### config.go

#### Config Loading
- [x] TestDefault - existing
- [ ] TestLoadFromFile - load valid TOML config
- [ ] TestLoadFromFileInvalid - malformed TOML
- [ ] TestLoadFromFileMissing - handles missing file
- [ ] TestLoadFromEnvVar - NTM_CONFIG_FILE env var
- [ ] TestLoadPrecedence - file > default

#### Config Paths
- [x] TestGetProjectDir - existing
- [x] TestGetProjectDirWithTilde - existing
- [ ] TestConfigFileLocations - check all search paths
- [ ] TestXDGConfigDir - XDG_CONFIG_HOME support

#### Agent Commands
- [ ] TestDefaultAgentCommands - default cc, cod, gmi
- [ ] TestCustomAgentCommands - override in config
- [ ] TestAgentCommandEnvVars - environment variable support

#### Tmux Settings
- [ ] TestDefaultTmuxSettings - default panes, palette key
- [ ] TestCustomTmuxSettings - override in config

### palette.go (palette loading)

#### Palette Loading
- [x] TestDefaultPaletteCategories - existing
- [ ] TestLoadPaletteFromTOML - [[palette]] entries
- [ ] TestLoadPaletteFromMarkdown - command_palette.md
- [ ] TestPalettePrecedence - file > TOML > defaults
- [ ] TestPaletteMarkdownFormat - ## Category, ### key | Label
- [ ] TestPaletteCommentsIgnored - # lines skipped

#### Palette Content
- [ ] TestPalettePromptContent - prompt text captured
- [ ] TestPaletteCategoryGrouping - commands grouped correctly
- [ ] TestPaletteKeyUniqueness - duplicate keys handled

## Test Helpers
```go
func createTempConfig(t *testing.T, content string) string {
    f, _ := os.CreateTemp("", "ntm-config-*.toml")
    f.WriteString(content)
    f.Close()
    t.Cleanup(func() { os.Remove(f.Name()) })
    return f.Name()
}
